plainMeaning: Let us also beg forgiveness of the teacher of the four-month retreat, the guru who reads and explains the scriptures to us.
recitation: comāsī gurune khamāvīe re, vāñce sūtra siddhānta

wordByWord.gloss: of the Chaumāsī (the four-month rainy-season retreat); i.e. the guru who leads the Cāturmāsa
wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati comāsī < Skt. cāturmāsa (catur 'four' + māsa 'month'); the monsoon retreat in which ascetics halt and teach
wordByWord.notes: Spelled ચોમાસી (comāsī) verbatim here, beside the tradition's ચઉમાસી (cāumāsī); marks the occasion on which this verse is sung.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. guru 'weighty, venerable teacher' + Guj. dative postposition -ne

wordByWord.gloss: let us ask forgiveness of / let us appease
wordByWord.etymology: Causative of √kṣam 'to forbear, forgive' (Pkt. khamāvei, 'cause to forgive'); first-person plural exhortative
wordByWord.notes: The song's governing verb, here directed to the teaching-guru.

wordByWord.etymology: Old Gujarati / Apabhraṁśa exclamatory re, the metrical refrain-marker of the geet
wordByWord.notes: Carries the refrain pulse 'khamo bhavi khamana re'.

wordByWord.etymology: Guj. vāñcvũ 'to read'; in liturgical use 'to read out and explain to the assembly' (< Skt. √vañc / vācana 'recitation')
wordByWord.notes: Subject is the guru; relative-clause without explicit relative pronoun.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. sūtra 'thread' < √sīv/syū 'to sew'; a terse text 'stringing together' doctrine

wordByWord.gloss: the siddhānta (established doctrine; here the Āgama corpus)
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. siddha 'proven, accomplished' (√sidh) + anta 'end, conclusion' = 'settled final teaching'
wordByWord.notes: In Jain usage siddhānta commonly denotes the Āgamas themselves; paired with sūtra as 'scripture-and-its-doctrine'.

literalTranslation: The Chaumāsī guru — let us ask his forgiveness, O! — who reads out the sūtra and the siddhānta.
idiomaticTranslation: And the guru of the four-month retreat, O fortunate soul — let us beg his pardon too: he who reads aloud and expounds to us the sūtra and the established doctrine.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: The song's descent of reverence reaches its final, intimate object: not a remote rank of beings but the living teacher seated before the assembly during the Cāturmāsa. Having appeased Jinas, preceptors, monks, lay-folk, the whole Sangha and the two great founder-acharyas, the worshipper now turns to the very guru from whose mouth this knowledge flows. The verse identifies him by his single defining act — 'vāñce sūtra siddhānta', 'he reads out scripture and doctrine'. The pairing is deliberate: sūtra is the terse received text, siddhānta its settled meaning (in Jain idiom, the Āgamas as a body); together they name the complete gift of śruta the guru hands down. To ask forgiveness of this person is the most concrete moment in the geet, for he is the one a reciter has actually wronged through inattention, doubt, or disrespect at the lectern. The line is marked in the book for the Chaumāsī occasion, sung with the resident teacher in view, and so seals the khamaṇā where it began — in an immediate human relationship rather than an abstraction. The forgiveness-song thus ends not on lineage grandeur but on the humblest and most consequential debt: that owed to the one who teaches.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse foregrounds jñānācāra and guru-vandana — right conduct toward knowledge and its giver. In Jain ethics, offence against the source of śruta-jñāna (the scripture-teacher) is counted gravely, for liberating knowledge reaches the soul only through the guru's vācana. Asking his pardon repairs the eight aticāras of knowledge (disrespect of text, teacher, time, etc.) implicitly transgressed during study. By coupling sūtra with siddhānta the verse affirms that scripture is inseparable from the authoritative doctrine that interprets it — one venerates not the letter alone but the living transmission.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Recited at a season-end Cāturmāsa pratikramaṇa, this line invites the practitioner to look directly at the teacher who has instructed them through the four months and to release any irritation, pride, or inattention shown during discourses — making the abstract litany of forgiveness land on a concrete, present relationship.

crossReferences.text: Muni Vandanā / guruvandanā with the Anchalgachchha paṭṭāvalī (reverence to teachers)
crossReferences.source: Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha ed.), muni-vandana

crossReferences.text: Mannaha Jiṇāṇaṁ Sajjhāy — the śrāvaka's daily duty of guru-vandana and śravaṇa (hearing scripture)
crossReferences.source: Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit, mannaha-jinanam-sajjhay

crossReferences.text: khāmemi savve jīve, savve jīvā khamantu me — the universal forgiveness formula underlying the geet

sources.consultedFor: Etymology and senses of sūtra (thread/aphoristic scripture) and siddhānta (established doctrine/conclusion); root √kṣam for khamāvīe

sources.title: GujaratiLexicon — Gujarati dictionary
sources.consultedFor: Glosses of the vernacular verbs khamāvavũ ('to ask forgiveness, appease') and vāñcvũ ('to read, recite/expound')

sources.title: Chaturmas (Cāturmāsya) — Wikipedia
sources.consultedFor: Meaning and timing of the four-month monsoon retreat that defines the 'comāsī guru' and the season-end Cāturmāsī observance

sources.consultedFor: Identity of the Vidhipaksha/Anchalgachchha tradition and its founder Aryarakshitsuri, locating the khamaṇā's lineage frame

notes: The source line gives only the first (opening) line of verse 7; the song's refrain 'khamo bhavi khamana re' is supplied by the recurring pattern and not repeated here. ચોમાસી is transliterated comāsī verbatim (the tradition KB elsewhere uses cāumāsī). The verse is asterisked in the book and sung on the Cāturmāsī occasion with the resident teacher named/intended; the dative ગુરુને and relative clause વાંચે...are read as 'the comāsī guru who reads/expounds sūtra and siddhānta'. Word-separation in the recitation is editorial for chanting; siddhānta is taken in its standard Jain sense of the Āgama doctrine.
